title-group.article-title: A Canonical Quaternionic Intermediate Representation for Single-Qubit Quantum Circuits

abstract.p: This paper defines a canonical quaternionic intermediate representation for single-qubit circuit segments. Building on the fixed quaternion-SU(2) correspondence established in qqc-001-foundations, it treats the physically relevant action of a single-qubit gate modulo global phase by first choosing an SU(2) representative and then representing that representative by a unit quaternion q = w + x i + y j + z k. The paper introduces a compiler-facing IR object u1q(w, x, y, z), formalizes its unit-norm and sign-ambiguity constraints, and defines deterministic normalization and canonicalization rules. The canonical representative is chosen from {q, -q} by a closed-hemisphere convention w > 0, together with an explicit equatorial tie-break when w = 0. This yields a shortest-geodesic, deterministic internal form suitable for fusion and rewrite pipelines. Explicit translations are given for the identity, Pauli gates, H, S, T and their adjoints, and axis rotations Rx(theta), Ry(theta), Rz(theta). Composition of adjacent single-qubit gates is shown to correspond to quaternion multiplication followed by re-canonicalization, and worked examples illustrate the relation between named gate syntax, canonical IR, and single-qubit segments extracted from larger circuits. The scope is limited to single-qubit gate action modulo global phase; no multi-qubit IR, optimization guarantee, or hardware claim is made.

license.license-p: This draft is distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License.

article-meta.related-object: Named gates to quaternion IR figure

sec.title: 1. Introduction
sec.p: This paper moves the series from mathematical language into software-architecture language by defining a deterministic internal representation for single-qubit circuit structure. The target is not frontend syntax but a canonical representation layer for compiler pipelines.

Named gate syntax is useful at the program boundary, but it is not itself a canonical representation of single-qubit action modulo global phase. The purpose of the present paper is therefore to define a quaternionic payload and a deterministic canonicalization rule.

sec.title: 2. Background and dependency on Foundations of Quaternionic Quantum Computing
sec.p: The paper inherits from Foundations of Quaternionic Quantum Computing the facts that single-qubit gates live in U(2), that they admit SU(2) representatives after removal of global phase, and that the unit quaternions model SU(2) exactly under a fixed convention Phi.
Under that convention the only residual ambiguity in the quaternion model is the sign pair {q, -q}. This is the ambiguity resolved by the canonicalization rule defined here.

sec.title: 3. Definition of the canonical quaternionic IR
sec.p: The paper defines a quaternionic single-qubit IR element u1q(w, x, y, z) as a unit quaternion q = w + x i + y j + z k interpreted as an SU(2) representative of single-qubit gate action modulo global phase.
It also defines a single-qubit segment as a maximal consecutive run of gates on one wire between circuit endpoints or boundaries such as entangling gates that leave the single-qubit unitary model.

sec.title: 4.1 Normalization
sec.p: A nonzero quaternionic quadruple is first normalized back to unit norm to control floating-point drift.

sec.title: 4.2 Sign canonicalization
sec.p: The canonical hemisphere is chosen by w > 0. On the equator w = 0, the sign is determined by the rule that the first nonzero component among (x, y, z) must be positive. This yields a unique representative of each class {q, -q}.

sec.title: 5. Translation from named gates into quaternion IR
sec.p: The paper gives explicit canonical tuples for I, X, Y, Z, H, S, S-dagger, T, T-dagger, and for the rotation families Rx(theta), Ry(theta), and Rz(theta). The translation layer is semantic rather than syntactic: different frontend spellings may map to the same canonical tuple.

sec.title: 6. Composition and segment representation
sec.p: Adjacent single-qubit gates compose by quaternion multiplication before re-canonicalization. If q_1, ..., q_m are the unit-quaternion representatives of gates g_1, ..., g_m in application order, then the segment payload is Can(q_m ... q_1).
Entangling gates are not represented internally by this IR; they serve only as boundaries that split single-qubit segments.

sec.title: 7. Worked examples
sec.p: The paper works through examples such as H ; T ; Rz(pi/3), Rx(pi/2) ; Ry(pi/2), and Z ; S ; T, together with a two-wire example showing how a CNOT boundary separates single-qubit segments without bringing multi-qubit structure into the IR itself.

sec.title: 8. Standard mathematics vs RQM Technologies framing
sec.p: The standard mathematical content concerns U(2), SU(2), the unit quaternions, the sign ambiguity q ~ -q, and quaternion multiplication. The project-specific contribution is the IR design choice: the u1q object, the hemisphere convention, the equatorial tie-break, and the segment-fusion framing.

sec.title: 9. Scope, limitations, and non-claims
sec.p: This paper does not define a full multi-qubit quaternionic IR, does not specify the internal semantics of entangling gates, does not prove optimization advantage, and does not replace existing SDKs or backend formats. It defines only a canonical representation layer for single-qubit gate action modulo global phase.

sec.title: 10. Conclusion
sec.p: The paper provides a deterministic bridge from quaternionic single-qubit geometry to compiler IR design. A single-qubit gate segment can be represented canonically as u1q(w, x, y, z), with normalization and sign-resolution rules that preserve the exact physical action modulo global phase while remaining implementation-friendly.

ref.mixed-citation: Cross, Andrew W.; Javadi-Abhari, Ali; Alexander, Thomas; de Beaudrap, Niel; Bishop, Lev S.; Heidel, Steven; Ryan, Colm A.; Sivarajah, Prasahnt; Smolin, John A.; Gambetta, Jay M.; Johnson, Blake R. OpenQASM 3: A Broader and Deeper Quantum Assembly Language. ACM Transactions on Quantum Computing. 2022; 3(3):12. doi:10.1145/3505636
Hall, Brian C. Lie Groups, Lie Algebras, and Representations: An Elementary Introduction. 2nd ed. Springer; 2015.
Kuipers, Jack B. Quaternions and Rotation Sequences: A Primer with Applications to Orbits, Aerospace, and Virtual Reality. Princeton University Press; 1999.
Nielsen, Michael A.; Chuang, Isaac L. Quantum Computation and Quantum Information. 10th Anniversary Edition. Cambridge University Press; 2010.
Smith, Robert S.; Curtis, Michael J.; Zeng, William J. A Practical Quantum Instruction Set Architecture. arXiv. 2016. arXiv:1608.03355
